data.title: Airbus Q1 profit cut in half as aircraft deliveries slowed to lowest since 2009
data.description:
Airbus (EADSF) (EADSY) down 1.6% in Tuesday's trading after reporting a sharp ​decline in Q1 core profit on Tuesday, falling well below ‌market expectations as the planemaker delivered fewer aircraft and was hurt by a weaker dollar.

Q1 adjusted EBIT plunged 52% Y/Y to €300M (~$351M), down from €624M in the year-earlier quarter and well below FactSet expectations of €378M, while sales fell 7% Y/Y to €12.65B ($14.82B), slightly above the €12.58B consensus.

Airbus (EADSF) (EADSY) confirmed its full-year delivery target, saying it is on track to ship ~870 commercial aircraft to customers this year, vs. 793 it shipped in 2025 - a sign of confidence that it can assemble enough planes despite an engine shortage that forced it to slow production of its best-selling A320 jets.

The company has faulted Pratt & Whitney for failing to meet contractual obligations, leaving it at a disadvantage just as rival Boeing continues to improve production.

Airbus (EADSF) (EADSY) reported cash outflow of €2.5B in the quarter, compared with a €310M drop a year earlier, because of the lower aircraft delivery and a planned buildup of inventory as it prepares for higher output.

The company had already disclosed that it delivered 114 commercial aircraft in Q1, down from 136 aircraft in the same quarter last year, meaning it would need to send ~756 units in the three remaining quarters this year to meet its goal.

data.title: American Tower raises 2026 outlook by $0.12 per share, reiterates 200-300 bps tower margin expansion target by 2030
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: American Tower (AMT) Q1 2026

Management View

  • American Tower opened the year by raising full-year guidance, with CEO Steven Vondran framing it as: "Our performance through the early part of the year, combined with favorable FX and straight-line dynamics led us to raise our full year outlook."
  • Vondran reiterated 2026 priorities and positioned towers and CoreSite against long-duration demand drivers, saying: "Rising wireless data consumption, accelerating cloud adoption, rapidly expanding AI-driven workloads and future generational technology shifts, all point towards sustained investment in high-quality digital infrastructure."
  • On towers and CoreSite growth assumptions, Vondran said the company is targeting "approximately 4% organic tenant billings growth across our global tower portfolio when adjusting for onetime DISH-related impacts and double-digit growth from our data center business," and described CoreSite momentum as: "this quarter marked a clear inflection in interconnection activity, enhancing both the profitability of the platform and the long-term durability of customer relationships."
  • CFO Rodney Smith summarized Q1 performance and the drivers behind the guidance increase: "our strong performance, coupled with FX and straight-line tailwinds have led us to raise our full year outlook."

Outlook

  • Smith raised full-year property revenue outlook "by approximately $145 million at the midpoint" ("approximately $110 million of FX tailwinds" and "approximately $35 million of accelerated noncash straight-line revenue in Latin America related to Oi"), while reiterating regional organic growth assumptions and full-year expectations for "organic tenant billings growth of approximately 1% or approximately 4% when excluding DISH churn and data center growth of approximately 13% year-over-year."
  • Smith raised adjusted EBITDA outlook "by approximately $105 million at the midpoint" and raised attributable AFFO outlook "by $0.12 per share," adding that attributable AFFO per share growth on an FX-neutral basis is expected to be "faster in the back half of the year than the front half" due to maintenance capital and cash tax timing.
  • Longer-term growth framing in Q&A centered on AFFO durability after event-driven items, with Smith stating: "The outlook for 2026 is in line with our longer-term view for AFFO per share growth, which is up in the mid-single digits to better than mid-single digits before you account for the impacts of FX and interest rates."

Financial Results

  • Smith said consolidated property revenue grew "approximately 3% year-over-year when excluding noncash straight-line revenue and FX impacts," and on a DISH-normalized basis "property revenue grew approximately 5% on a cash FX-neutral basis," driven by "organic tenant billings growth of approximately 2% or 4% normalized for the impact of onetime DISH churn" and "data center cash revenue growth of approximately 17%."
  • Smith reported profitability trends as: "Adjusted EBITDA grew 1% when excluding net straight-line and FX impacts," while "cash adjusted EBITDA margins declined approximately 110 basis points year-over-year, primarily due to DISH-related churn, SG&A timing and higher fuel prices in Africa."
  • By region, Smith cited organic tenant billings growth of "approximately 1%" in the U.S. and Canada ("approximately 5% when excluding DISH churn"), "approximately 11%" in Africa and APAC, "approximately 4%" in Europe, and Latin America down "approximately 2%, primarily driven by elevated churn in Brazil."

Q&A

  • Ric Prentiss, Raymond James: asked about EchoStar DISH/AT&T spectrum deal pace and whether an escrow path is being discussed; CEO Vondran responded: "We really can't comment on ongoing litigation" and added, "we believe our contract is enforceable" and "we've completely derisked our earnings and our guidance by taking DISH out of our numbers."
  • Ric Prentiss, Raymond James: asked about Europe new builds, contract structure, and returns; CFO Smith said Europe has "outperform[ed] the original business case" and expects returns "above our weighted average cost of capital in that region by a couple of hundred basis points over time," while Vondran added: "we are also winning some things that are outside the contract on very good terms because of our operational excellence."
  • Michael Rollins, Citi: asked how AMT is approaching M&A and whether it would consider U.S. public-to-public or public-to-private; Vondran said, "We have a very disciplined capital allocation formula" and "There's no sort of strategic imperative to overpay for anything." Rollins also asked about AFFO expectations beyond 2026; Smith pointed to "mid-single digits to upper single-digit AFFO and AFFO per share growth rate over time."
  • Eric Luebchow, Wells Fargo: asked about edge compute and whether CoreSite belongs in AMT longer term; Vondran said, "edge is absolutely something that we think is going to continue to grow" and added, "I do think it has a long-term place in our portfolio."
  • Cameron McVeigh, Morgan Stanley: asked about expanding vs. retrofitting CoreSite amid power and supply constraints; Vondran said the team now takes a "longer-term view" on land/power/components and is "being more aggressive," while Smith quantified capacity planning: CoreSite development held for development increased from "about 287 -- 280 megawatts" by "200 megawatts."
  • Brendan Lynch, Barclays: asked about Brazil normalization path and churn/new business; Smith said Latin America churn is "peaking" and expects organic tenant billings to move "from a negative OTBG into positive territory in the lower single digits in '27" with "normalized growth" by "'28 and beyond," while Vondran added: "We're absolutely seeing an increase in demand" in Brazil.
  • David Barden, New Street: asked about implications of rumored SBA privatization and how satellites could disrupt terrestrial networks; Vondran said, "we're not going to comment on the rumors" and on satellites: "Satellites are complementary to terrestrial networks" and "I don't lose a second sleep worried about satellites."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to pressing on headline risks and valuation cross-currents, with repeated probing on DISH uncertainty, private-market multiples, and potential disruption (e.g., Prentiss citing DISH process concerns and Barden asking, "What does it mean if SBA gets taken private?").
  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more guarded in legal-related Q&A, with firm repetition of de-risking language (Vondran: "we've completely derisked our earnings and our guidance") and confidence on strategy and CoreSite demand (Vondran: "The business has meaningfully exceeded our expectations").
  • Versus last quarter, Q1 maintained confidence but became more restrictive on DISH commentary; in Q4, Vondran emphasized the run-rate reset while still providing directional exposure context, whereas in Q1 he said, "we really can't comment" and leaned on the public docket.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from establishing a DISH-reset baseline in Q4 2025 to formally raising consolidated outlook in Q1 2026, with Smith attributing the increase to "incremental FX and straight-line tailwinds."
  • CoreSite narrative strengthened from Q4’s emphasis on "impressive double-digit growth" and AI-ready capacity to Q1’s "clear inflection in interconnection activity" and explicit willingness to "accelerat[e] CoreSite's expansion" as a larger value driver.
  • Analysts stayed focused on DISH and capital allocation in both quarters, but Q1 added heavier questioning on private-market valuation signals (SBA/take-private framing) and disruption narratives (SpaceX/satellites).

Risks and Concerns

  • DISH remains an overhang primarily through litigation uncertainty; Vondran said, "we really can't comment on ongoing litigation" but emphasized: "we've completely derisked our earnings and our guidance by taking DISH out of our numbers."
  • Latin America churn pressure, especially Brazil, continued to weigh on organic growth; Smith attributed the 2026 pattern to churn timing shifts and pointed to a recovery path into 2027 and normalized growth by 2028.
  • CoreSite development faces local permitting resistance; Vondran acknowledged, "we are seeing an increase" in NIMBY-related friction, while adding it has not forced project cancellations or "significant delays" so far.

Final Takeaway

Management’s Q1 message centered on a raised 2026 outlook driven mainly by FX and straight-line tailwinds, continued DISH de-risking with any DISH recovery framed as incremental upside, and an increasingly prominent CoreSite thesis built around accelerating AI-driven workloads and an interconnection inflection. Across Q&A, investors pressed on litigation opacity, capital allocation in a shifting public/private valuation landscape, Latin America churn timing, and disruption risk, while management leaned on disciplined return hurdles, balance-sheet flexibility, and the view that towers and CoreSite remain positioned for sustained demand.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: UHS reiterates 2026 outlook while targeting $800M-$900M in buybacks amid Talkspace integration
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Universal Health Services (UHS) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "The first quarter of 2026 feature significant acceleration in our behavioral health outpatient strategy with the announcement of the Talkspace acquisition" (CEO, President & Director Marc Miller), alongside "continued steady operating performance and cash flow generation" amid "more challenging seasonal volume trends" (CEO Miller).
  • "We expect the deal to be accretive to earnings during the first 12 months post-closing" and "By year 3 post-closing, we expect the effective EBITDA multiple for the Talkspace transaction to be in the single-digit range" (CEO Miller), with planned clinical expansion such as "virtual intensive outpatient programs or IOPs" (CEO Miller) and "bidirectional revenue synergy opportunities" (CEO Miller).
  • "Our enterprise-level AI governance process remains very active" and "We deployed and scaled a total of 8 different use cases of AI solutions into our revenue cycle operations that are now yielding significant benefit" (CEO Miller), while noting, "It is too early to project the longer-term financial impact of the 2026 initiatives" (CEO Miller).
  • "The company reported net income attributable to UHS per diluted share of $5.65" and "our adjusted EPS was $5.62 for the first quarter" (Executive VP, CFO & Secretary Steve Filton).

Outlook

  • "We are reiterating the financial and operating forecasts that we established on February 25" and "we plan to reevaluate annual guidance as necessary in conjunction with our second quarter earnings" (CFO Filton).
  • "We are reiterating the full year $75 million pre-tax impact" from health insurance exchange dynamics, and that forecast "assumes the exchange declines will steepen somewhat as the year progresses" (CFO Filton).
  • "In California, we are making good progress year-to-date with respect to the state's nurse staffing ratio requirements that go into effect June 1, and we remain on track with the assumptions contemplated in our 2026 outlook" (CFO Filton).

Financial results

  • "Revenue growth for the first quarter was 9.6%" and "Adjusted EBITDA net of NCI increased 8.4%" (CEO Miller).
  • "On a same-facility basis, adjusted admissions at our acute care hospitals were unchanged" and UHS "estimate acute care volumes during the first quarter of 2026 were impacted by approximately 200 basis points" from "weaker flu and respiratory activity and winter weather" (CFO Filton).
  • "Same-facility acute care emergency department visits increased approximately by 2%" and UHS cited strength in "cardiology, orthopedics and neurology" (CFO Filton).
  • "Net revenues in our acute care hospital segment during the first quarter of 2026 increased 8.2%" and "Acute care same-facility revenue per adjusted admission increased 6.3%" (CFO Filton), while noting a comparison impact from "approximately $30 million of prior period supplemental program net benefit" (CFO Filton).
  • "Same-facility contract labor was 2.3% of Acute Care segment revenues" (CFO Filton) and "Cash generated from operating activities was $402 million" with "$217 million on capital expenditures" (CFO Filton).
  • "Same-facility net revenues [behavioral] increased 7.3%" and "Same-facility behavioral health segment EBITDA increased by 8.4%" (CFO Filton), with "salaries, wages and benefits per adjusted patient day increased by approximately 6%" (CFO Filton).
  • "We acquired 675,000 of our shares at a total cost of $127 million" and had "$1.3 billion of repurchase authorization available" (CFO Filton), adding, "we expect to remain active with share repurchase throughout 2026" (CFO Filton).

Q&A

  • Albert Rice, UBS: Asked for acute core EBITDA excluding flu/weather and DPP swings; CFO Filton: "I think it was in the low single-digit range." Rice, UBS: Asked which AI use cases are most meaningful; CEO Miller: "we've deployed and scaled a total of 8 different use cases of AI solutions into our revenue cycle operations" and "we're also doing a lot of things that are touching the patient."
  • Jason Cassorla, Guggenheim: Asked about out-of-period Medicaid supplemental payments and the path to the full-year growth framework; CFO Filton: "everything that you suggested...was in our guidance" and cited drivers including "continued ramp-up of the new facilities" and that "we expect them both to improve as the year goes on" regarding volumes.
  • Ann Hynes, Mizuho: Asked on bad debt/collectibility amid exchange subsidy changes; CFO Filton: "we also recorded an additional reserve" and "we believe that our $75 million negative estimate...is good."
  • Matthew Gillmor, KeyBanc: Asked what drove stronger pricing and why it moderates; CFO Filton: "mix with a lower sort of flu component" and higher acuity service lines contributed; on professional fees: UHS is "largely operating within" "the single-digit range...even if it's in the high single digits" and is using "more RFPs" and fewer locums.
  • Joanna Gajuk, BofA: Asked on payer mix and supplemental programs; CFO Filton: "slight increase in uninsured" and on Florida supplemental payments: "high level of confidence" and UHS had been estimating "about a $50 million benefit" (CFO Filton), while California was "much less certain" (CFO Filton).
  • Justin Lake, Wolfe: Pressed that "core EBITDA" looked down ex-DPP; CFO Filton: "we understood that our earnings trajectory would have to increase" and "still believe that we're going to get there for the full year."
  • Andrew Mok, Barclays: Asked for March/April volume progression; CFO Filton: "March was...a cleaner month" and "March volumes...showed a more normative increase."
  • Scott Fidel, Goldman Sachs: Asked on behavioral demand/supply and capital allocation amid outpatient and Talkspace; CFO Filton: "behavioral demand remains strong" and is shifting to outpatient, and buybacks remain compelling with a target "in the $800 million to $900 million range" while noting Talkspace "does not increase our leverage significantly" (CFO Filton).

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts: Slightly negative-to-skeptical on core earnings quality and moving parts, with direct challenges such as "it looks like core EBITDA was down" (Justin Lake, Wolfe) and questions on bad debt and exchange exposure (Ann Hynes, Mizuho).
  • Management: Neutral-to-slightly positive, emphasizing expectations were embedded and reiterating outlook, including "nothing...was outside of our expectations" and "our overall results were within our expectations" (CFO Filton), while framing Talkspace as "a multi-year value creation opportunity" (CEO Miller).
  • Versus last quarter, Q1 discussion kept a similar framing on AI and outpatient expansion, but analyst tone in Q1 was more concentrated on parsing "core" performance and DPP/exchange normalization; management responses included reaffirmations like "we are reiterating" guidance (CFO Filton) and acknowledgments such as "we're not at that core 5% growth" in the quarter (CFO Filton).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Strategy: Q1 introduced the Talkspace deal as a new strategic catalyst, with CEO Miller calling it "the industry's first end-to-end continuum of behavioral health care services"; in Q4, CEO Miller highlighted building outpatient footprint via "Thousand Branches Wellness" and opening "at least 10 more Branches locations during 2026."
  • Guidance language: Q1 maintained the Q4 stance of annual guidance stability, with CFO Filton saying UHS is "reiterating the financial and operating forecasts"; Q4 had set 2026 ranges including revenue "$18.4 billion" to "$18.8 billion" and adjusted EPS "$22.64" to "$24.52" (CFO Filton).
  • Key debates: Q1 Q&A shifted toward separating DPP and seasonal impacts from underlying EBITDA and tracking exchange-related bad debt reserves; Q4 Q&A focused more on setting assumptions (exchange declines, California staffing costs) and the ability to achieve the 2% to 3% volume framework.

Risks and concerns

  • "We estimate an impact of approximately $15 million" in Q1 from health insurance exchange trends and reiterated the full-year exchange headwind of "$75 million pre-tax" (CFO Filton), with additional reserve risk from members who "will not sustain their premium payments" (CFO Filton).
  • Seasonal disruption risk remained central, with UHS estimating acute volumes were hit by "approximately 200 basis points" from weaker flu/respiratory activity and winter weather (CFO Filton).
  • Regulatory cost risk in California was reiterated, with UHS stating it remains "on track" for staffing ratio compliance effective June 1 (CFO Filton).

Final takeaway

Management characterized Q1 as an on-plan quarter operationally despite flu, weather, and exchange-related headwinds, while positioning the Talkspace acquisition as a near-term earnings accretive step that accelerates UHS’ outpatient behavioral continuum. The company reiterated its 2026 outlook framework, emphasized ongoing revenue-cycle and AI-driven efficiency work, and maintained an active capital return stance, including continuing share repurchases alongside funding de novo capacity expansion and the pending Talkspace close.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Federal Reserve is expected to keep rates unchanged ahead of Fed chair transition
data.description:
As the Federal Open Market Committee meets on Tuesday and Wednesday to decide on monetary policy, the interest rate decision will be the most expected item on the list of Federal Reserve watchers’ items of interest. 

The central bank decision is being made as Fed Chair Jerome Powell helms his last meeting with Fed Chair nominee Kevin Warsh waiting in the wings. President Donald Trump continues to pressure the Fed head to cut rates.  And the FOMC will be struggling to assess whether the oil shock triggered by the Iran conflict will bleed into underlying inflation trends.

The question looming over the meeting will be whether Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell will serve out his term as a Fed governor after his term as chair ends. Now that the Department of Justice has dropped its investigation of Powell’s handling of the Fed’s renovation project, former Fed Governor Warsh is poised to be confirmed as Fed chair by the time Powell’s chair term ends on May 15. 

Powell can serve two more years as a board governor, though, a move that would break with the tradition of leaving the board after a chair’s term expires if he decides to stay.

“The most important part of this meeting is: What is Jay Powell going to do?  And what hints he’s going to give as to whether he stays on,” said Heather Long, chief economist at Navy Federal Credit Union, in an interview with Seeking Alpha. “It seems likely that he will stay on as a Fed governor, at least for a while.”

She’s most interested in Powell’s take on where the investigation stands. “The DOJ has dropped it, but he used those terms that he wouldn’t leave until it was fully over, and it’s not fully over,” Long said. “It’s just in somebody else’s hands.”

After the last FOMC meeting, Powell said, "I have no intention of leaving" the board until the Department of Justice investigation is "well and truly over." US Attorney for the District of Columbia Jeanine Pirro said she reserves the right to restart the DOJ investigations if the facts warrant it. That could leave enough doubt in Powell’s mind to hold onto his Fed governor slot. And the investigation hasn’t so much resolved as shifted to another overseer. The Inspector General of the Federal Reserve is taking over the probe to ascertain whether the Fed chair managed the project properly and whether his testimony to Congress violated any laws.

Regarding the interest rate decision, traders are pricing in a 100% probability of no change to the current 3.50%-3.75% federal funds rate range, according to the CME FedWatch tool. Even more telling, the market is pricing in no rate change for the rest of the year, compared with the start of 2026, when traders were pricing in two 25 basis-point rate cuts.

Navy Federal’s Long now expects that the central bank will reduce its benchmark rate at its December meeting. That contrasts with expectations before the Iran war, when traders were pricing in a rate cut in June or July. That hope has now vanished as the conflict led to surging oil prices.

As always, traders, economists, and other Fed watchers will be focused on any forward guidance the FOMC may provide in its statement. In the minutes for the March meeting, there was some debate on whether its statement should include language that the next rate move could be either a cut or a hike.

While it’s possible there could be more discussion of that at this meeting, Long doesn’t expect it to be in the statement. “From what I'm reading and hearing, I think the bar is very, very high, and that they would not be positioning the Fed well if they went with that language,” she said.

One reason to keep the federal funds rate target rate unchanged is the economic uncertainty. Most Fed officials have said they're in no rush to cut rates until they have a clearer picture of how the Iran war will affect inflation. With long-term inflation expectations remaining well anchored, there's no urgent need to adjust rates. Another major reason is the upcoming succession. "You don't want to rock the boat before you're handing the Fed over to the new chair," Long said.

And it's important to Powell that he hand the Fed over to his successor with policy in a "good place." "I think he's built up a lot of trust in the markets and how he passes the baton," Long said.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Oil rally accelerates on UAE OPEC exit, and these 10 energy stocks have taken notice
data.description:
Energy stocks led U.S. equities higher Tuesday, emerging as the top-performing segment in the S&P 500 (SP500) after a sharp move in crude prices.

Oil (CL1:COM) climbed 3.5% to trade just below the $100-per-barrel mark, reflecting renewed supply uncertainty. The catalyst came from the United Arab Emirates’ decision to exit OPEC, a development that has stirred concerns about cohesion within the producer group and future output coordination.

Investors interpreted the move as potentially supportive of tighter global supply conditions, lifting sentiment across the oil and gas space and driving broad-based gains among exploration, production, and midstream companies. 

Outlined below are the top 10 performing S&P 500 energy stocks on Tuesday: 

  1. Coterra Energy (CTRA), +2.9%.
  2. Occidental Petroleum (OXY), +2.9%.
  3. ONEOK (OKE), +2.9%.
  4. Targa Resources (TRGP), +2.8%.
  5. Devon Energy (DVN), +2.8%.
  6. Kinder Morgan (KMI), +2.7%. 
  7. Exxon Mobil (XOM), +2.5%. 
  8. Chevron (CVX), +2.4%. 
  9. EQT Corporation (EQT), +2.3%.
  10. Williams Companies (WMB), +2.3%. 

Energy ETFs: (XLE), (VDE), (XOP), (OIH), (AMLP), and (IXC). 

Oil ETFs: (USO), (UCO), (DBO), (OILK), and (USL).

Natural Gas ETFs: (UNG), (BOIL), and (UNL).
